Surveys:
Village Survey and house hold survey
The Unnat Bharat Abhiyan team of IIT Jammu started village survey in the month of
November 2019 along with the NSS volunteers of IIT Jammu. The baseline survey started
with the villages Tanda, Jahrri and Jagti of Jagti Panchayat and village Panjgrain of
Panjgrain panchayat of Jammu district. The village survey was followed by house hold
survey of Tanda and Jarri and Jagti. During the surveys the team found the major problems
faced by the villagers:
● Road connectivity
● Unaware of Govt. schemes like , old age pension, widow pension, kissan benefit
schemes of central and state governments
● Unemployment
● Sanitation, garbage stagnation
● No govt. or private schools nearby
● No public transport
All these problems were noted down for the solutions.
Visit of psychiatrists to village Jarri and Jagti:
During survey the team identified two cases of seviour Pshcho problems and both are girls,
one from Jarri village and another one from Jagti village. UBA team arranged the
appointments of two psychiatrists Dr. Abhiruchi Chatterji (community Psychiarist) and Dr.
Bhaskar Mukherjee (Neuroscience specialist) who happened to visit IIT Jammu during that
period. The UBA team along with these eminent doctors visited both patients for treatment
and prescribed the medicines and followup route.
Truck Drivers Survey stranded on NH-44 near our campus:
A survey of about 75 stranded truck drivers was done in the month January 2020 by NSS
volunteers and UBA team to know about their habits of living on the roads and the
difficulties they face to maintain their health and hygiene.
By the survey we came to know that there are almost no facilities available on National
highways.
----No facility of drinking water, washrooms, bathing etc.
----They choose playing cards, smoking, listening loud music, using Internet
during stranded period to come out of boredom and to pass time
----They go in open fields to deface
----they often drink and misbehave with the people around

Response to Covid-19, awareness levels in the village and how the villages withstood challenges posed by COVID-19 Introduction In response to pandemic caused by SARS-COV-2 (i.e. COVID-19) our social responsibility as an academic institute of higher education has given us a pristine opportunity to work for the betterment of the society that we are a part of. IIT Jammu adopted 5 villages so far under Unnat Bharat Abhiyan, with almost 6500 population, coming under three Panchayats. We did a small study to understand the current situation of our adopted villages. We collected information to understand the awareness level, practice, challenges and how the villages withstood challenges posed by COVID-19. Methodology Information collected and verified through key-informants interview and field visits. Discussion Table1. Socio-economic condition and mode of awareness In the initial phase of lock down UBA team of IIT Jammu went to villages of Panjgrain panchayat and distributed masks and spread awareness through door to door visit. In the surrounding villages a good number of working people are dependent on IIT Jammu for their living. During our visit to villages we observed, police and volunteers from villagers (high school students and youth) are strictly maintaining surveillance and in shops, buyers are also keeping social distance. Health situation We also collected information about elderly (see table 2 for details). Till now they are getting help from the police and other villagers to access health facilities outside villages. Though villagers are maintaining social distances and other prescribed norms, knowledge and understanding about the virus is not sound. Markets remain open only once during the morning for four hours. Table 2. Health condition and response to Covid-19 during lockdown Apart from State Government and panchayat initiatives some NGOs like Sewa Bharati, SAT India, and Khadi Gram Sabha are also helping these villagers by distributing food and necessary goods in the villages apart from two villages of Panjgrain and Jarri. However, these services are not regular and not evenly distributed.15 Families got bank Payment in their account in advance for the gas cylinder. Migrant labourers In the surrounding villages construction workers of IIT and IIM Jammu are staying and they couldn't go back to their native place. So far we have identified around 175 migrant families along with a large number of single migrant men (see table 3 for details). Many people are going through the crisis. Until now they got some edible oil,

Amount distributed by panchayats is not even in all these villages. Table 2. Situation of Migrant workers and response to Covid-19 during lockdown Challenges: 1) It is observed and shared by villagers that those who are staying near roads with good social networks are getting benefits from Govt. and NGOs and those who are staying in the interior or uphill are not getting any help like the Gujjar’s, in Jarri villages. Food stock they had for they are almost over and they are yet to get outside help till now. 2) Most of the villagers, daily wage labourers dependent upon outside help, only received 15 days' food so far and getting further help is uncertain. 3) Migrant labourers, who are mostly from UP, Bihar and Jharkhand want to go back to their respective villages. However, they on and off are getting little support from the contractor with whom they are working. 4) Govt. school students are mainly from humble backgrounds and facing problems with their class. As Govt. schools, here in Jammu, mostly do not have infrastructure to support online classes and many times students don't have access to mobile or computer unlike private school students. Teachers are giving them assignments and notes which for many find a challenging way to learn. 5) So far, no tests were done for COVID-19 though few elderly, the most vulnerable age group, with their caregivers, are regularly visiting the city hospital for their treatment. IIT Jammu’s response In this regard the students of IIT Jammu under the NSS program and social welfare club have started some innovative initiatives that are as follows: 1 Distribution of food for Migrant workers The students are managing daily distribution of food to average 31 Migrant workers, from UP, Jharkhand and Bihar involved in construction works and stuck at IIT Jammu, as well as spreading awareness about COVID-19 among them. Leading by example, the students are also demonstrating the necessary precautions that need to be taken in order to limit the spread of the disease, as prescribed by the government and Medical Unit of IIT Jammu. 2 Online lecture series for Govt. School students Other than this, the students are also in the process of preparing lectures for 8th,9th and 10th classes for mathematics and sciences courses. Currently two teams are working on this. The first team is preparing course materials, whereas the other team is working on video that would effectively be able to convey the concepts to the target audience. Their major focus is on government school students who are severely affected by this lockdown. Additionally, the students plan to make these videos in Hindi and English so that it is accessible to the majority of students. Later, the plan is to recreate the same video in other native languages to reach more students. This will involve students who speak the respective languages from the cohort. 3. Myth Busting and Information update On similar motivation, a four member team is involved in developing an App for spreading awareness on COVID-19. The App will contain recent updates regarding: 1. Newly added cases both state wise and approved by the central government 2. Update on any advisory related to the same issued by the government officials. 3. Contain myth busting videos, under guidelines of WHO and MOHW. These are some basic points on which the prototype app will be based. Currently the students are working on the building platform. Later, we plan to add more features for other diseases as well. 4. We are working to help these villages as well, with the limited resources that we have without jeopardizing the safety of all involved. I would like to thank Mrs. Jeyoti Kaul for her help and coordination for the same. The migrant workers from other states were in need of ration. It was observed by the villagers that those who are near the main roads were getting these benefits, however, those who are staying in the interior or uphill were not getting any essential supplies. 2. Lack of transportation has led to a crisis situation for small scale farmers and florists. Most of them are unable to send their produce/flowers to the market. This is resulting in heavy losses for their livelihood. 3. Additionally, there have been reports of improper cultivation practices being followed due to the pandemic situation. 4. The pandemic situation has gravely affected the single women householders who run a living based on daily wages. Due to lack of day work, they are unable to provide for their families. 5. One of the greatest point of concern is the lack of COVID-19 testing for villages. So far no tests were done in any of these villages. 6. Shortage of masks is another issue. In this

During that crucial period the UBA team tried to build communication with the village women and whom we find self-motivated were ready to work. The team went on trying and with the continuous communication, these women formed the self-help group “Jai Laxmi Mata” of 8 members, which they started with the collection of mere 100 rupees per member per month and opened the group bank account too, where Mrs. Poonam Devi is President of SHG, Sunita Devi, Secretary and others are members of the SHG. The UBA team went with the concept of Mask making, which was the need of hour. Initially we tried simple templates as a trial and improvement process will continue. Joshi (Padma Bhushan) from IIT Mumbai delivered an online Public Lecture on "Prosperity of India through Science and Technology”. The lecture was organised by Department of Chemical Engineering IIT Jammu in association with UBA-IIT Jammu and NSS-IIT Jammu on 151th birth anniversary of Mahatma Gandhi on 2nd October 2020 between 3.30 p.m. to 5.30 p.m. Prof. Joshi while sharing his knowledge and expertise guided the participants that we as technical institutions shall be the creators of sprouting knowledge and design cutting edge technologies that will have the greatest impact on society and benefit the mankind at large. He further suggested that the leading global Universities, Institutions shall provide transformative education to create leaders, innovators to generate new knowledge. While summarizing his lecture, he said that although we have received political freedom in 1947, but still we are more than 90% dependent in terms of technologies and new knowledge therefore we need to begin with yet another freedom fight for own abundant knowledge, innovations and

Making and then Sanitising the Masks: - In order to meet the first two challenges, the UBA team with the help of Sarpanch, Panch and villagers identified the local villagers who could make masks and gave them an opportunity to earn in this hour of crisis. These local villagers were identified and given raw material to make the masks. The masks after being prepared will be thoroughly sanitized and then distributed to Primary Health Care Centres of the village. In this way, the mask will reach the needy and requirement of the villagers will also be met. The sanitising material will also be given to all involved in making these masks. The villagers involved will be paid their remuneration which will be a stress buster for them in the current situation of lockdown and no daily income. 2. Dissemination of Authentic Information via. Hoardings, Hanging Charts etc.: Not all elderly have an access to the electronic media and in this hour of ongoing nationwide lockdown, it has become mandatory to take the health and hygiene of all (specially our old age population) more seriously. In an effort to disseminate authentic information, the UBA team also planned to hang information giving charts, several sign/picture hoardings along with the help of Jammu Municipal Corporation workers who move around for collecting waste or carrying fumigation drives as seen in Fig- 4 The primary work of making chart, relevant drawing, pictures etc. has already been done by the students of Government Polytechnic Jammu. The only task left at hand is to make a hoarding or flex out of these collected drawing. This task seems a bit stalled at the moment owning to the closure of several non-essential shops. Development of Online Health Service App: - The faculties of Government Polytechnic have developed an online app named “Dr4uOnline” to help sick people who cannot make it to the hospital during this peak time of COVID-19. This app books the appointment of a person with a doctor. The patient can speak to the doctor and ask for suitable medicines for himself. This application has been made by Rameez Rashid, faculty of Computer Engineering Department of Government Polytechnic Jammu along with Shakeel Ahmad, Deputy Registrar, J&K State Board of Technical Education. Till date more than 300 people have registered for consultation with the Doctor and more than 250 patients have already been treated successfully. Roy (UBA, PI Coordinator, IIT Jammu) Report of Vegetable dyeing and Eco-printing workshop On the occasion of International Women’s Day (8thMarch 2021). IIT Jammu’s Unnat Bharat Abhiyan (UBA) Cell, in association with NSS wing and Ek Bharat Shrestha Bharat (EBSB) club organized a workshop on Vegetable Dyeing and Eco printing to help set up the second Self Help Group (SHG) for fabricating vegetable dyed and eco-printed cloth at Tanda village Jammu. Vegetable dyeing and eco printing uses naturally occurring fauna to create and transfer print to fabric without the use of artificial inks, paints and chemicals (so no drawing or painting skills needed). The raw material used for the workshop was as follows: Silk and cotton fabric samples were used print media, marigold flowers, lantana flowers, mageeth twigs were used as colour source and alum and vinegar were used as fixing agents. The main objective of the workshop is to give the SHG beneficiaries training skill, so that they can be motivated to set up vegetable dyeing and printing units. The skill thus developed from dyeing and printing, the fabric can be stitched into artifacts like scarfs, pencil boxes, table mats, napkins etc. to generate income. The same was done in an effort to empower women in local villages and help them become entrepreneurs. 15 ladies from two villages, Jagti and Tanda of Jagti Panchayat along with students of IIT Jammu 2nd, 3rd and 4th year from different branches participated in the event. The training was imparted in Binna Craft, a local and traditional craft, which has become a dying craft due to the explosion of plastic articles. This craft is made by wrapping method and the raw material used is locally available natural resource, the date leaves (Patha) and the bushy grass. These leaves are sometimes used to dye, just to create design patterns. There is a customary importance of these articles during marriages even now. The Chabri (big tray) in this craft is used to put the Grooms Sahrah and Brides Chura (bangles) in Dogra marriages. The Craft got its name Binna, as in dogri, the binna means the article to sit on. Earlier the bride used to get these binnas while leaving parent’s house and were used as Aasan (seating covers) for performing Pooja etc. But now the Artisans are motivated and trained to create variety of daily utility item in this craft, just to increase demand to generate employment. Daily refreshment was provided to the beneficiaries. Raw Material Used: Patha, Dried Grass, Patha Colour Patha is the local name of date leaf, these leaves are collected and dried in bright day sunlight for 6 to 7 days. Computer Literacy Program of our Security Personnel The UBA and Outreach team is pleased to present the report on the Computer Literacy Program conducted for our security personnel. This program aimed to enhance the digital skills and overall computer literacy of our security team, ensuring they are well-equipped to handle modern technological challenges in the ever-evolving security landscape. The duration of the Program was 20 working hours, covering the following topics - Google software(Gmail, map, you tube, google meet, Translator, drive, photos) Microsoft Word, Power point, Canva, Microsoft Excel, Cyber Security etc. The primary goal of the program was to empower our security personnel with fundamental computer skills, enabling them to utilize technology efficiently in their daily tasks and contribute to enhanced security measures. It is a perennial plant whose oleo gum resin is extracted from its thick roots and rhizome. The plant stores most of its nutrients inside its deep fleshy roots. It is endemic to Iran and Afghanistan, which are also the main global suppliers of it. It is very popular in India and is used in cooking. It thrives in dry and cold desert conditions. The plant can withstand a maximum temperature between 35 and 40 degree, whereas during winters, it can survive in temperatures up to minus 4 degree. Regions with sandy soil, very little moisture and annual rainfall of not more than 200mm are considered conducive for heeng cultivation. During extreme weather, the plant can get dormant.Heeng is not cultivated in India. India imports about 1,200 tonnes of raw heeng worth Rs. 600 crore from Iran, Afghanistan and Uzbekistan. Moreover, this initiative is designed to generate employment opportunities within the region, through the cultivation, harvesting, processing, and marketing of bamboo products. Adopted Villages IIT Jammu recently conducted a large-scale household survey as part of the Unnat Bharat Abhiyan (UBA), reinforcing its commitment to rural development. Under the guidance of Prof. Manoj Singh Gaur, Director, IIT Jammu and Prof. Abhay Sharma, Dean and Head RCI, the initiative aimed to gather crucial data for community development. The initiative commenced with a formal training session, led by Dr. Nalin Sharma, PI Coordinator, which provided volunteers with standardized survey methodologies, ethical guidelines, and data management protocols, ensuring rigorous data collection. Subsequently, survey conducted in three villages: On March 8th, a team of 24 NSS volunteers surveyed approximately 67 households in the Panjgrain Panchayat, Dansal Block, with data promptly uploaded to the UBA Portal. On March 22nd, 23 volunteers, in collaboration with local representatives, surveyed all three wards of Pangali village, Chibba Panchayat, located approximately 10 kilometres from the IIT campus. 77 households were surveyed, and the collected data is slated for upload to the UBA portal. On March 29th, 20 volunteers conducted a survey of 65 households in Jagti village of Jagti Panchayat, Dansal Block. The data is currently being uploaded. These meticulously organized surveys, facilitated by robust logistical support and adherence to UBA protocols, yielded critical data for evidence-based development planning, reflecting IIT Jammu’s dedication to impactful community engagement and data-driven initiatives. The volunteer support was provided by the students of IIT Jammu under Kritash Club, headed by Dr. Ravikant.

Assistance in Establishing AI/Robotics Lab at APS Nagrota: On 25 June 2025, a team from IIT Jammu visited Army Public School (APS) Nagrota to assess the existing AI Lab facilities and suggest curriculum enhancements. The team met with the Vice-Principal and AI instructors to discuss aligning practical components with current industry standards. The possibility of future instructor training by IIT Jammu faculty and technical staff was also proposed. Following the visit, an internal call for volunteers was initiated. Three lab engineers—Dr. Vikrant Guleria, Mr. Approve, and Mr. Vikas—volunteered to extend support on weekends. A planning meeting was held on 8 September 2025 under the guidance of the Associate Dean (Outreach), involving the volunteer team, school instructors, and the outreach office. Subsequently, the volunteers conducted a detailed assessment of the existing laboratory infrastructure. Based on their evaluation, they submitted a list of required equipment and developed comprehensive AI curricula tailored separately for junior and senior students. The volunteer team conducted weekend classes at the school, which concluded successfully. The initiative received positive feedback from the school authorities, and contacts were exchanged to ensure continued support and future collaboration as required. Training of SEG Proposal – On-site at Kuwara Village, Panjgrain: On April 16, 2025, a hands-on training session was conducted for rural women in Kuwara Village, Panjgrain. The training focused on mold assembly, wax pouring, and candle designing, with emphasis on practical learning and entrepreneurial skill development. The initiative aims to establish a sustainable and replicable model for economic empowerment. ## Centre for Essential Skills (CES)

Vision and Mission of CES, IIT Jammu

## Vision of CES – IIT Jammu

The Centre for Essential Skills (CES) at Indian Institute of Technology Jammu was established with the vision of
creating an inclusive and forward-looking learning ecosystem centred on science, technology, and innovation.
The Centre aims to promote holistic education and skill development by nurturing intellectual curiosity,
scientific thinking, creativity, and ethical leadership among learners from diverse academic backgrounds.

CES seeks to complement technical education with experiential and application-oriented learning that responds to
contemporary industrial and societal requirements. Alongside strengthening scientific and technological
capabilities, the Centre emphasizes the development of design thinking, financial awareness, communication and
language skills, professional competencies, and other essential life skills within an integrated educational
framework.

By encouraging interdisciplinary engagement and practical exposure, CES aspires to equip learners with critical
thinking abilities, adaptability, socio-economic awareness, and entrepreneurial orientation necessary to
navigate
the challenges of a rapidly changing global environment.

The Centre is committed to strengthening capacity-building initiatives for students, educators, professionals,
institutions, and communities, with a special focus on creating meaningful impact across the regions of Jammu &
Kashmir and Ladakh. By fostering collaboration between academia, industry, government, and society, CES aims to
contribute towards innovation-driven growth, inclusive development, and the national vision of building a
Viksit Bharat.

## Mission Statements of CES

🎓

To promote excellence in science, technology, and innovation through interdisciplinary and experiential
learning approaches that integrate academic knowledge with real-world applications.

🧠

To develop intellectual curiosity, scientific temper, creativity, ethical values, and leadership qualities
among learners from diverse educational and professional backgrounds.

🧩

To strengthen essential skills including communication, design thinking, financial literacy, technical
competencies, problem-solving abilities, and entrepreneurial orientation for holistic personal and
professional growth.

🤝

To foster meaningful collaboration among academia, industry, government, and society in order to address
emerging industrial, technological, and socio-economic challenges.

🌱

To undertake capacity-building and outreach initiatives that contribute to inclusive and innovation-driven
development, with particular emphasis on the regions of Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh, while supporting the
national vision of a Viksit Bharat.

🇮🇳

### Viksit Bharat Vision

To support the vision of a self-reliant and developed India by nurturing capable, ethical, and
future-ready citizens for a Viksit Bharat.

The Outreach and Skilling Division (OSD) at IIT Jammu acts as a dynamic platform for
meaningful engagement with a diverse spectrum of stakeholders. Our initiatives are
thoughtfully designed to introduce young learners to emerging technologies, strengthen the

larger national aspiration of building a Viksit Bharat.

Heeng Cultivation in Ladakh UT, India: ------ a success story by Collaborative Efforts under UBA network by IIT Jammu Background: Heeng is a herbaceous plant of the umbelliferae family. It is a perennial plant whose oleo gum resin is extracted from its thick roots and rhizome. The plant stores most of its nutrients inside its deep fleshy roots. It is endemic to Iran and Afghanistan, which are also the main global suppliers of it. It is very popular in India and is used in cooking. It thrives in dry and cold desert conditions. The plant can withstand a maximum temperature between 35 and 40 degree, whereas during winters, it can survive in temperatures up to minus 4 degree. Regions with sandy soil, very little moisture and annual rainfall of not more than 200mm are considered conducive for heeng cultivation. During extreme weather, the plant can get dormant.Heeng is not cultivated in India. India imports about 1,200 tonnes of raw heeng worth Rs. 600 crore from Iran, Afghanistan and Uzbekistan. In 2017, IHBT approached the National Bureau of Plant Genetic Resources (NBPGR) with an experimental project idea to cultivate heeng in the Indian Himalayas. The agriculture ministry of Himachal Pradesh has identified four locations in the Lahul-Spiti valley and has distributed heeng seeds to farmers in the region and is working perfectly and has not yet matured as it takes five years to mature. Every year the sampling are grown and the scientists are expected the gum next year. Kargil and Drass areas of Ladakh UT’s climatic and soil conditions are similar to that of conditions Lahul-Spiti valley of Himachal Pradesh. Therefore to take up this task forward, the UBA family/Network took up this mammoth task of putting this into reality on Indian soil at Kargil. The collaborative efforts among various institutions under the Unnat Bharat Abhiyan (UBA) Scheme, aimed at empowering rural communities like those in Kargil. that. Post Heeng farmers training An online meeting for ‘Launch of Heeng Cultivation’ in Kargil was held on 1st April 2024 . After the successful training of Kargil based farmers in heeng cultivation at IHBT Palampur between 4th March to 6th March 2024. An online meeting between all the collaborative agencies was kept for the implementation / launch the Heeng (Asafoetida) cultivation in the UBA adopted villages of one of our PI GDC Kargil. This event marks a significant milestone in our efforts to promote sustainable agriculture and empower local farmers in the region. NIScPR Delhi under the leadership of Prof Yogesh Suman and UBA NCI team motivated CSIR-IHBT Palampur for providing 300 heeng plants free of cost as part of training program for the launch to the farmers and farmers are supposed to carry the sampling from the IHBT Palampur before 4 to 6 days of launch. IHBT will issue the material after two signed copies of MTA. The Material Transfer Agreement (MTA) is to be signed between IHBT Palampur as first party and Government Degree College, Kargil as 2nd party, because the Govt. Degree College being a Participating Institution for UBA, who have initiated this event and will be the monitoring agency of the heeng cultivation till it gives a first crop. The meeting was attended by all the collaborative agencies and the farmers on 1st April 2024. The main agenda was to get the land ready for the cultivation as per the ration given during the training as well as in the meeting, the plants are to be planted at 1 x 1 m spacing. Therefore, space requirement will be approximately 300 sq.m. and for transportation the farmer representative need to bring plastic crates of standard size, one crate carries 12 plants. Accordingly, 25 crates will be required. It was decided that theses saplings will be carried only in a heavy vehicle and the Kargil Manali road for heavy vehicles open in 1st or 2nd week of May. Thus we are planing accordingly. They were joined by the newly appointed Project In charge (P.I.) of Unnat Bharat Abhiyan, Mr. Syed Ali Mousavi. Post Plantation:
From May to early November 2024, the plants demonstrated vigorous growth.
Subsequently, in accordance with their established dormancy cycle, as confirmed
by IHBT Palampur scientists, they entered a period of winter quiescence,
characterized by leaf desiccation. The recent emergence of new sprouts signifies
the successful completion of this dormant phase. This resumption of growth is a
positive indicator of the plants' resilience and viability, and it is welcomed by all
stakeholders involved in the project.

Palli Residential Energy Utilization and Waste Generation Survey by Regional Coordinating Institute Indian Institute of Technology Jammu Palli, a small hamlet located in the Samba district of UT of Jammu and Kashmir, made history on 24th April 2022 when honorable Prime Minister of India, Sh. Narendra Modi declared the panchayat as India’s first carbon-neutral panchayat. Keeping in line with this vision, Ministry of Panchayati Raj and Samba district administration deputed the work of surveying and analyzing the residential energy utilization and waste generation in Palli panchayat to the Unnat Bharat Abhiyan (UBA) Regional Coordinating Institute (RCI): Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Jammu. The first meeting was called by Ministry of Panchayati Raj and state administration of Samba and the same was attended by the Unnat Bharat Abhiyan RCI team of IIT Jammu headed by Dr. Gaurav Bhaduri, Dr. Prasun Haldar and UBA staff Mrs. Jeyoti Kaul. This meeting was also attended by MoPR officials and state administration. Some of the dignitaries from all over India including Prof. V K Vijay, National Coordinator, Unnat Bharat Abhiyan. This report presents the details of the conducted survey and the analyzed outcomes for assessment of residential energy utilization and waste generation in Palli panchayat. The Participating Institutes (PIs): Dogra Degree College and Government Polytechnic Jammu under RCI IIT Jammu volunteered to conduct the field survey. Under the guidance of Mr. Balwant Singh, PI Coordinator, Dogra Degree College, and Mr. Subhash, PI Coordinator, Government Polytechnic Jammu, two teams of 23 and 18 students, respectively, conducted the ‘Energy Survey’ and ‘Waste Survey’ of 724 households in the entire panchayat. The team of IIT Jammu then first accomplished the mammoth task of digitizing the entire data from the physical copies of the form. This was done with the help of 16 student volunteers from IIT Jammu under the guidance of Prof. Rakesh Singhai, Dean, Outreach and Continuing Education Programs, and RCI Coordinator, IIT Jammu. After the digitization of the data, it was compiled and analyzed thoroughly by Dr. Riya Bhowmik, Asst. Prof. Civil Engg. Dept., and Associate Dean, Outreach and Skill Development Programs and Mrs. Jeyoti Kaul, Project Staff, UBA, IIT Jammu. The present report provides detailed information about the state of energy access, energy use patterns, and various modes of waste generation in Palli homes. The analyzed records of survey were presented before the state administration by Dr. Riya Bhowmik. Another Survey for Vehicular GHG (CO2)

All 641 household heads were called and asked for their details of personal or commercial vehicles and their corresponding usage per month. The survey took nearly half a month to complete. Success Story-2
SHG-2: Sacha Peer Baba
Activity: Binna Craft (Local Art)
The second SHG was created after our team during the survey realized that most of the local
ladies make baskets, Chabri’s (trays) in local binna craft, but very few can make it up to the
market, hence a two week training program in Binna craft was started on 15 November
th
2021 in village Tanda of Jagti Panchayat (UBA adopted village) by Office of Continuing
Education Program and Unnat Bharat Abhiyan wings of IIT Jammu in association with
National Rural Livelihood Mission (NRLM). The training was imparted in Binna Craft, a local
and traditional craft, which has become a dying craft due to the explosion of plastic articles.
This craft is made by wrapping method and the raw material used is locally available natural
resources, the date leaves (Patha) and the bushy grass. These leaves are sometimes used to
dye, just to create design patterns. There is a customary importance of these articles during
marriages even now. The Chabri (big tray) in this craft is used to put the Groom's Sahrah and
Brides Chura (bangles) in Dogra marriages. The Craft got its name Binna, as in dogri, the
binna means the article to sit on. Earlier the bride used to get these binna’s while leaving
parents’ house and were used as Aasan (seating covers) for performing Pooja etc. But now
the Artisans are motivated and trained to create a variety of daily utility items in this craft,
just to increase demand to generate employment
Raw Material Used:
Patha, Dried Grass, Patha Colour
Patha is the local name of date leaf, these leaves are collected and dried in bright day sunlight
for 6 to 7 days. Some of the dried leaves are dyed to create design pattern in the articles while
weaving. IIT Jammu has added as a marketing source by using these binna articles as
souvenirs and gifts for the guests during events and seminars etc. the repeated orders have
encouraged the SHG to review the dying art.

## Unnat Bharat Abhiyan

Unnat Bharat Abhiyan is an important flagship program of Ministry of Education, Govt. of India.

## Vision of Outreach – IIT Jammu

The Outreach Program at IIT Jammu serves as a bridge between the Institute and society,
aiming to inspire, inform, and empower students, particularly from underserved,
remote, and border regions of Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh.

Since 2024, IIT Jammu has hosted over **10,000 school students**
through routine requests, including students from government and far-flung schools. The program seeks to raise
awareness about science, technology, and higher education while fostering curiosity and ambition among students
with limited exposure to such opportunities.

## Key Components of Outreach Department

🔬

### Inspiration & Awareness

Exposure to advanced laboratories, research activities,
and real-world applications of science and engineering.

🧪

### Hands-on Learning

Live demonstrations and guided lab visits to complement classroom learning.

💬

### Interactive Engagement

Regular faculty–student interactions to encourage inquiry and academic guidance.

🎓

### Mentorship & Counseling

Support for exploring STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) career pathways.

👨‍🎓

### Student Interaction

Engagement with IIT Jammu students to promote peer learning and motivation.

🌏

### Inclusive Outreach

Focus on students from remote and government schools to ensure equitable access.

👩‍🔬

### Vigyan Jyoti Scheme

Encouragement of meritorious girl students to pursue STEM education and promote gender equity.

## Objectives

• To inspire students through exposure to advanced laboratories and real-world applications of science and
engineering.

• To enhance learning through hands-on demonstrations and guided lab visits.

• To promote interactive faculty–student engagement for academic guidance and inquiry.

• To provide mentorship for exploring STEM career pathways.

• To encourage peer learning through interaction with IIT Jammu students.

• To ensure inclusive outreach to students from remote and government schools.

• To support the Vigyan Jyoti Scheme for promoting STEM education among meritorious girl students.

## Vision of Unnat Bharat Abhiyan

Unnat Bharat Abhiyan is a flagship initiative of the Ministry of Education, Government of India, envisioned to
bring transformational change in rural India through the active participation of Higher Education Institutions
(HEIs). The vision of the program is to create a sustainable and inclusive development model by connecting HEIs
with nearby villages, enabling the application of knowledge, innovation, and appropriate technologies to address
local development challenges.

It seeks to foster a meaningful partnership between academia and rural communities, where students and faculty
engage directly with grassroots realities, understand socio-economic issues, and contribute to evidence-based
solutions. In alignment with this vision, greater involvement of institutional students and faculty is essential
to deeply engage with adopted villages, identify real-world challenges, and develop impactful SEG proposals for
funding support from the Ministry. Strengthening such engagement under the UBA network will enhance capacity
building, promote experiential learning, and accelerate holistic rural development.

## UBA Institutional Roles

🌍

### Regional Coordinating Institution (RCI)

Coordinates UBA activities across Jammu Province and the Union Territory of Ladakh

Provides guidance and mentorship to participating institutions

Strengthens regional-level implementation of the program

Supports initiatives for local product development and local culture preservation through institutional
collaboration and knowledge sharing

🏡

### Participating Institution (PI)

Adopts villages for development activities

Identifies and addresses local problems

Applies innovation, research, and technology for rural development solutions

Promotes local product development and preservation of local culture through community engagement and
sustainable intervention
